# Transformer KV Cache Optimization

In transformer-based language models, the key-value (KV) cache is a fundamental optimization for autoregressive generation. During inference, the model stores computed key and value matrices from attention layers for all previously processed tokens. Without this cache, generating each new token would require recomputing attention states for the entire preceding sequence, resulting in quadratic computational complexity. By caching these intermediate states, each new token generation only requires computing attention with respect to the cached states plus the new token, yielding significant speedup.

The KV cache grows linearly with sequence length and the number of attention layers, making memory management a central concern for long-context inference. Several strategies address this pressure. Attention sink methods retain a small set of initial tokens alongside a sliding window of recent tokens, exploiting the empirical observation that early tokens accumulate disproportionate attention mass regardless of semantic relevance. Structured caching approaches organize cached states to support efficient retrieval over long or graph-structured contexts rather than treating the cache as a flat sequential buffer. Memory allocation strategies such as paged attention partition the KV cache into fixed-size blocks that can be allocated and freed dynamically, reducing fragmentation and enabling more efficient GPU memory utilization during batched inference.

For proprietary models accessed via API, where direct KV cache control is unavailable, theoretical KV cache costs can be estimated by analyzing the final conversation state and categorizing tokens as input versus output according to official tokenization schemes. For open-weight models deployed with serving frameworks such as vLLM, KV cache benefits can be directly measured and tuned. Experiments confirm that while multi-turn conversations increase average input token counts due to history accumulation, inference efficiency in terms of latency and throughput remains stable thanks to cache reuse.

To manage context growth in long multi-turn conversations, compaction operators such as deterministic summarization can compress windows of conversation turns into shorter representations while preserving essential information. These operators trade a one-time summarization cost for reduced cache size and faster subsequent attention computation, and can be designed to maintain behavioral equivalence with the uncompacted conversation under appropriate conditions.

Research on KV caching also extends to latent collaboration approaches where agents or system components communicate through shared latent representations rather than visible text tokens, potentially reducing the number of tokens that must be cached while preserving information flow.

## See Also

- [[z20260318-036|KV Cache Sharing in Homogeneous Multi-Agent Workflows]] — applies KV cache reuse principles to the specific setting of single-LLM simulation of multi-agent systems
